---
title: "Chart Toppers"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Gavin Caldwell"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/music_presentation_background.jpg
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
    footer: '[Project Home](index.html)'
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

# Load packages here
pacman::p_load(tidymodels,
               tidyverse,
               here,
               lubridate,
               stringr,
               scales,
               patchwork)

```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Plot theme
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11))

# For better figure resolution
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.retina = 3, 
  dpi = 300, 
  fig.width = 6, 
  fig.asp = 0.618 
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false
# Load data here
# load dataset

data(mtcars)
mtcars$speed <- mtcars$hp / mtcars$wt

data("penguins")

# read in csv for each question
billboard <- read.csv(here("data", "billboard.csv")) |>
  mutate(year = year(ymd_hms(date))) |> # convert to year 
  filter(year != 2025) # filter out 2025 (only one song)
```

# Introduction {background-image="images/music_presentation_background.jpg"}

## Introduction {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

- How has the American Music Industry changed in the last 60 years?

- Dataset of every #1 Song on Billboard Hot 100 from 1958 to 2024

- Curated by Chris Dalla Riva^1^

- Uploaded to the TidyTuesday 2025 project^2^ by Jen Richmond^3^

## The Data {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

- A glimpse at the data:

```{r}
#| label: glimpse-the-data
#| message: FALSE

glimpse(billboard)

```

## Motivations {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

::: incremental
- I have always enjoyed music
- Interested in the history of music and genre trends
- Curious how centralized the American Music Industry is, and what effect, if any, it has on songwriting
:::

## The Questions {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

:::: incremental
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

- "Has the diversity of record labels that produce American popular music decreased over time?"

  - Record Labels "time-share" of #1 Songs

  - Parent Companies "time-share" of #1 Songs

- "Has American popular music grown more homogeneous over time?"

  - BPM

  - Song Length

  - Musical Key

  - Number of Key Changes in Songs
  
:::
::::

# Record/Parent Label Diversity {background-image="images/music_presentation_background.jpg"}

## Record Labels Share of #1 Songs {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
#| label: label-viz
#| warning: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE

label_data <- billboard |> # data wrangling
  select("weeks_at_number_one", "year", "label", "parent_label") |> # isolating variables
  separate_rows(label, sep = ";") 


# Find labels with fewer than 52 total weeks across all years
small_labels <- label_data |>
  group_by(label) |>
  summarise(
    total_weeks = sum(weeks_at_number_one),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(total_weeks < 52) |>
  pull(label)

# filter out small labels and then proportions plot across all years
record_label <- label_data |>
  mutate(year_group = (year %/% 5) * 5) |>
  mutate(year_label = paste0(year_group, "-", year_group + 4)) |>
  mutate(label = ifelse(label %in% small_labels, "Independent", "Top 15")) |>
  mutate(label = fct_relevel(label, "Independent", after = Inf)) |>
  group_by(year_label, label) |>
  summarise(
    weeks_at_number_one = sum(weeks_at_number_one),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) 

ggplot(record_label, aes(x = year_label, y = weeks_at_number_one, fill = label)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.5,
    color = "red",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = -Inf,
    y = 0.5,
    label = "50% of Weeks",
    hjust = -0.3,
    vjust = -0.5,
    color = "red"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(),
    name = "Percent of Weeks at Number One"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Record Labels Time at #1 Song",
    subtitle = "Meaured by weeks, grouped every 5 years",
    caption = "From 1958 to 2024",
    x = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
- Measured by Weeks Every 5 years
- Top 15 Record Labels Grouped Together
- Any record label with less than one year share of #1 Song spot over 60 years labeled as "Independent"
:::
:::::

## Parent Labels Share of #1 Songs {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
#| label: parent-viz
#| warning: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE

parent_label_data <- label_data |>
  mutate(year_group = (year %/% 5) * 5) |>
  mutate(year_label = paste0(year_group, "-", year_group + 4)) |>
  add_count(parent_label, name = "count") |> # getting count of variables
  mutate(parent_label = ifelse(
    year_group == max(year_group), # if last group
    case_when( # special labeling for last group year
      parent_label == "Sony" ~ parent_label,
      parent_label == "Vivendi" ~ parent_label,
      parent_label == "Warner Bros." ~ parent_label,
      parent_label == "Universal" ~ parent_label,
      TRUE ~ "Independent"
    ),
    ifelse(count < 104, "Independent", parent_label) # normal rule
  )) |>
  mutate(parent_label = fct_relevel(parent_label, "Independent", after = Inf)) |>
  group_by(year_label, parent_label) |>
  summarise(
    weeks_at_number_one = sum(weeks_at_number_one),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) 

ggplot(parent_label_data, aes(x = year_label, y = weeks_at_number_one, fill = parent_label)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.25,
    color = "red",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = -Inf,
    y = 0.25,
    label = "25% of Weeks",
    hjust = -0.3,
    vjust = -0.5,
    color = "red"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(),
    name = "Percent of Weeks at Number One"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Parent Companies Time at #1 Song",
    subtitle = "Meaured by weeks, grouped every 5 years",
    caption = "From 1958 to 2024",
    x = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)    
  )

```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}
- Measured by Weeks Every 5 years
- Top 4 Parent Companies Identified
- Any parent company with less than 2 years share of #1 Song spot over 60 years labeled as "Independent"
- Exception is Universal, who spun off from Vivendi in 2021^4^
:::
:::
:::::

# #1 Song Musical Homogeneity {background-image="images/music_presentation_background.jpg"}

## BPM and Song Length Standard Deviation {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}

```{r}
#| label: bpm-viz
#| warning: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE

# read in data
bpm_length <- billboard |> # data wrangling
  select("song", "year", "bpm", "length_sec") |> # isolating variables
  pivot_longer( # pivoting longer for bpm and song length
    cols = c(bpm, length_sec),
    names_to = "category",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  group_by(year, category) |> # grouping by year and category for Standard Deviation category
  summarise(sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE)) # calculating standard deviation

ggplot(bpm_length, aes(x = year, y = sd, color = category)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(
    ~ category, 
    ncol = 1,
    labeller = as_labeller(c(
      bpm = "Beats Per Minute",
      length_sec = "Song Length"
    ))) + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Standard Deviation of Each #1 Song's BPM and Length",
    subtitle = "Standard Deviations Calculated per Year",
    caption = "From 1958 to 2024",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Standard Deviation"
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

```

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

- BPM and Song Length Standard Deviation
- Trend Line Added
- Reported for every year

:::
:::::

## Musical Key Frequency and Key Changes {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: keys-viz
#| warning: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 8

# count number of songs per year
songs_per_year <- billboard |>
  mutate(year_group = (year %/% 5) * 5) |>
  mutate(year_label = paste0(year_group, "-", year_group + 4)) |>
  distinct(song, year_label) |>
  count(year_label, name = "n_songs")

# count the use of keys
keys_count <- billboard |>
  select("song", "year", "keys") |> # isolating variables
  mutate(year_group = (year %/% 5) * 5) |> # Group every 5 years
  mutate(year_label = paste0(year_group, "-", year_group + 4)) |>
  mutate(keys = str_remove_all(keys, "&")) |>
  mutate(keys = str_remove_all(keys, "%")) |> # cleaning up keys variable
  separate_rows(keys, sep = ";") |> # enumerate all keys used in song
  count(year_label, keys, name = "n_keys") |> # count the times a key was used in a given year 
  complete(year_label, keys, fill = list(n_keys = 0)) |> # fill in missing keys with 0 for plotting purposes
  mutate(
    keys = factor(
      keys,
      levels = c(
        "Abm", "Ab", "Am", "A",
        "Bbm", "Bb", "Bm", "B",
        "Cm", "C", 
        "Dbm", "Db", "Dm", "D",
        "Ebm", "Eb", "Em", "E",
        "Fm", "F",
        "Gbm", "Gb", "Gm", "G")
    )
  )

# normalize key count per year
keys_count_norm <- keys_count |>
  left_join(songs_per_year, by = "year_label") |>
  mutate(prop = n_keys / n_songs)
  
ggplot(keys_count_norm, aes(x = year_label, y = keys, fill = prop)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "cividis", labels = label_percent()) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 8.5,
    color = "red",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 8.6,   
    y = 23,
    label = "1995",
    color = "red",
    angle = 0,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  labs(
    fill = "Proportion of Songs\nin that Key",
    title = "Frequency of Musical Keys in #1 Songs",
    subtitle = "Grouped Every 5 Years, Normalized to the Amount of #1 Songs in each Group",
    caption = "From 1958 to 2024",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Musical Key\n(Descending Order)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )


```

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
- Heatmap of Keys Used Every Year
- Diversity of key selection increases after 1995
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: multiple-keys-viz
#| warning: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 8

# count the use of multiple keys
multiple_keys_count <- billboard |>
  select("song", "year", "simplified_key") |> # isolating variables
  group_by(year) |>
  summarize(multiple_keys = sum(
    simplified_key == "Multiple Keys", 
    na.rm = TRUE)
  )

ggplot(multiple_keys_count, aes(x =  year, y = multiple_keys)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Times Multiple Keys are Used in #1 Songs",
    subtitle = "Counted per Year",
    caption = "From 1958 to 2024",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of Times Multiple Keys are Used"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank()
  )
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
- Number of #1 Songs using multiple keys decreases over time 
:::

:::
::::

# Conclusions {background-image="images/music_presentation_background.jpg"}

## Conclusions {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

:::: incremental

- The results of these visualizations leave the original idea of this proposal in an interesting place
- It does appear that record labels and especially parent companies are becoming more centralized
- But does not appear to have a quantifiable effect on songwriting
- More insights needed on what the effects of a centralized American Music Industry are

::::

## References {background-image="images/elegant-white-background-with-blue-wave-lines.jpg"}

1.  Chris's Website: [Can't Get Much Higher](https://www.cantgetmuchhigher.com/)
2.  Tidy Tuesday 2025: [Billboard Hot 100 Number Ones](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-08-26/readme.md)
3.  Jen Richmond: [Github Page](https://github.com/jenrichmond)
4.  CNBC Article: [Describing Universal's spin off from Vivendi](https://www.cnbc.com/2021/06/22/vivendi-awaits-key-shareholder-vote-on-universal-music-group-spin-off.html?msockid=26f73f417e226c542cfc29717f5d6d8d)

# Thank you! {background-image="images/music_presentation_background.jpg"}

# Using Quarto for presentations

## Quarto

- The presentation is created using the Quarto CLI

- `##` sets the start of a new slide

## Layouts

You can use plain text

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
- or bullet points[^1]
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
or in two columns
:::
:::::

[^1]: And add footnotes

- like

- this

## Code

```{r, echo=FALSE}
model <- lm(mpg ~ speed, data = mtcars) 

model |> tidy()

model |> glance()

```

## Plots

```{r}
penguins |>
  mutate(species = ifelse(species == "Adelie", "Adelie", "Other")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point()
```

## Plot and text

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Some text

- goes here
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, warning=FALSE, fig.width=5.5}
penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = species, color = species)) +
  geom_boxplot(linewidth = 0.75,
               outlier.size = 2.5) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  theme(legend.key.size = unit(0.8, "cm"))
```
:::
:::::

# A new section...

## Tables

If you want to generate a table, make sure it is in the HTML format (instead of Markdown or other formats), e.g.,

```{r}
penguins |> 
  head() |>
  kableExtra::kable() |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

## Math Expressions {.smaller}

You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. \$\\alpha+\\beta\$ renders $\alpha + \beta$. You can use the display style with double dollar signs:

```         
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
```

$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$

Limitations:

1.  The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting `$$` must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending `$$` must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;

2.  There should not be spaces after the opening `$` or before the closing `$`.

# Wrap up

## Feeling adventurous?

- You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that's what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.

- But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.
